refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure and report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined productivity target. The latter is a business outcome dependent on operational execution, workforce skill, site conditions, and maintenance—not solely a system design obligation. The refined FR isolates the system's functional responsibility: providing accurate, timely throughput data that stakeholders (Site Manager, Equipment Operator, Tele-operator) can use to assess performance against targets. This is solution-neutral (does not prescribe sensors, data pipelines, or display mechanisms) and atomic (single obligation: enable measurement and reporting).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct representative operations over the agreed evaluation period. Collect system-reported throughput metrics (m³/h or ton/h) and cross-validate against independent ground-truth measurement (e.g., weighbridge, volume surveys, manual tallies). Confirm measurement accuracy, latency, and availability meet acceptance criteria. Verify that reported data is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass) from operational sensors or manual input.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired data.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Store and retrieve throughput metrics with sufficient temporal resolution for performance analysis.
FR-NGHE-001.4: Display or report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Equipment Operator, Tele-operator) in real time or near-real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input or output) over time and derive throughput from level differential and known geometry
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous flow sensing) with reconciliation logic to cross-validate throughput estimate
DP5: Instrument material handling equipment (conveyor, pump, loader) with duty-cycle and capacity telemetry, then compute throughput from equipment utilization and known per-cycle material quantity
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor placement and material stream geometry: continuous sensing requires stable, representative access to the material flow; poor placement introduces systematic bias and couples measurement accuracy to site layout

Calibration and environmental drift: continuous sensors (density, velocity, mass flow) are sensitive to material property variation (moisture, particle size, temperature); drift over evaluation period must be managed independently of reporting logic

Integration method and time-series noise: numerical integration of noisy instantaneous flow introduces error accumulation; filtering and smoothing logic must be decoupled from the core throughput calculation to allow independent validation
Evaluation period definition: throughput target is time-dependent; clock synchronization and period boundary handling must not couple measurement acquisition to reporting schedule
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is preferred because it directly measures the phenomenon of interest (flow rate) without intermediate assumptions about batch size, equipment capacity, or reservoir geometry. Coupling risks are primarily environmental and operational (sensor placement, calibration) rather than logical. These can be managed through independent sensor validation protocols and environmental monitoring. DP2 and DP3 introduce stronger logical coupling: batch extrapolation assumes uniform batch size and regular intervals (fragile under operational variability); reservoir-based measurement couples throughput to geometry and level sensor accuracy. DP4 adds complexity without reducing fundamental measurement uncertainty. DP5 couples throughput to equipment duty cycle, which may not reflect actual material flow (e.g., partial loads, spillage, equipment inefficiency).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material stream is continuous or sufficiently frequent that instantaneous flow rate can be meaningfully sampled. Risk: Highly intermittent or batch-mode operations may require hybrid measurement (DP4) or fallback to batch counting (DP2).

Assumption: Sensor accuracy and stability are sufficient to meet productivity_target acceptance criterion over the evaluation period. Risk: Sensor drift, calibration loss, or environmental interference (temperature, humidity, material property change) could invalidate reported throughput. Mitigation: independent calibration checkpoints and environmental monitoring required.

Assumption: Integration method (numerical or analog) introduces acceptable error relative to target tolerance. Risk: High-frequency noise or long evaluation periods amplify integration error. Mitigation: specify filtering strategy and validation method (e.g., periodic manual spot checks) independent of continuous measurement.

Assumption: Evaluation period is well-defined and synchronized across measurement and reporting systems. Risk: Clock drift, period boundary ambiguity, or asynchronous data collection introduce systematic bias. Mitigation: explicit time-sync protocol and period definition in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Material throughput is the appropriate metric for productivity assessment. Risk: If actual productivity depends on material quality, processing time, or downstream utilization, throughput alone may not reflect operational success. Mitigation: confirm with stakeholders that throughput is the agreed KPI.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits continuous sensor installation and maintenance in the material stream. Risk: Sensor placement may conflict with safety zones, equipment access, or operational procedures. Mitigation: early coordination with safety and operations teams.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput (mass or volume per unit time) with accuracy sufficient to confirm that reported productivity meets or exceeds productivity_target over a defined evaluation period, subject to worksite safety and operational constraints. Measurement shall be independent of material property variation (moisture, density, particle size) and environmental drift, with documented calibration and validation protocol. Reporting shall include measurement uncertainty and any periods of sensor unavailability or recalibration.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition subsystems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and local cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) at periodic intervals

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period duration and flow measurement configuration parameters from Teleoperation or NGHE Solution

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link to transmit telemetry and receive tasking commands; may also receive real-time calibration or sensor diagnostics

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal conditioning, and local integration logic are naturally vehicle-resident. Diffusion occurs only at telemetry export boundary (measurement results transmitted externally). Sensor calibration and evaluation period parameters must cross boundary via Tasking interface.

Weak containment: NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer. Placing continuous sensing and real-time integration here creates tight coupling to vehicle hardware and introduces latency/reliability risks. Sensor data would need to flow from Vehicle to NGHE and back, violating locality of sensing.

Weak containment: Teleoperation is a command/control interface for human operators. Real-time continuous sensing and integration belong at the asset (Vehicle) level, not in the teleoperation layer. High diffusion: sensor hardware remains on Vehicle, creating split responsibility and remote computation latency.

Very weak containment: Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alerts. Flow rate measurement is a functional capability, not a safety function. Placing it here violates separation of concerns and creates unnecessary coupling to safety infrastructure.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of continuous flow rate sensing and cumulative throughput integration because: (1) sensing hardware is physically resident on the vehicle; (2) real-time signal conditioning and integration require low-latency local computation; (3) measurement results are a primary telemetry output; (4) external systems (Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) consume these measurements as inputs, not produce them. The DP is fully contained within Vehicle with minimal external diffusion (only measurement export via Telemetry interface and configuration input via Tasking interface).
containment_risks: Sensor calibration drift: if calibration parameters must be updated remotely, Tasking interface must support calibration command payloads; lack of bidirectional diagnostics may delay detection of sensor faults.
Evaluation period synchronization: Vehicle must receive evaluation period duration via Tasking; clock skew between Vehicle and external systems could cause integration window misalignment.

Telemetry bandwidth: high-frequency instantaneous flow rate samples may exceed available Network Link capacity; local buffering and downsampling logic must be contained within Vehicle to avoid external diffusion.
Offline operation: if Vehicle operates without Network Link, cumulative throughput must be stored locally and reconciled upon reconnection; this adds state management complexity to Vehicle boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable via the Vehicle's cumulative throughput computation and telemetry export. Verification requires: (1) independent ground-truth measurement of material throughput during representative operations, (2) comparison against Vehicle-reported cumulative value, (3) validation that evaluation period duration received via Tasking interface matches actual measurement window. Worksite safety plan integration is orthogonal to throughput measurement and does not impede verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated entirely to the Vehicle IM boundary. The DP (continuous sensing + integration) maps unambiguously to Vehicle-resident hardware and local computation. External systems (Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) consume measurement outputs but do not participate in the sensing or integration logic. Tasking and Telemetry interfaces are well-defined data contracts.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP correctly and completely addresses the FR. Continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) with integration over the evaluation period is the standard approach for throughput measurement. The DP is technology-agnostic (supports both mass and volumetric sensing) and logically sound. No functional gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Sensor calibration drift: Calibration parameters must be updateable via Tasking interface. If remote calibration commands are not explicitly supported in the Tasking payload schema, sensor drift will not be correctable without vehicle retrieval. Recommend: define Tasking interface to include calibration command type with parameter vectors.

Evaluation period synchronization: Vehicle clock must be synchronized with external tasking source to ensure integration window alignment. Clock skew >1% of evaluation period could cause systematic throughput underreporting. Recommend: Tasking interface include NTP-style timestamp or explicit start/stop markers; Vehicle must log actual integration window duration for audit.

Telemetry bandwidth and sampling rate: High-frequency instantaneous flow rate samples (e.g., 100 Hz) may saturate Network Link. If external systems require raw sample streams, diffusion occurs (Vehicle must buffer/downsample locally, but external systems dictate sampling policy). Recommend: define Telemetry interface with explicit sample rate and aggregation strategy (e.g., 1 Hz averaged samples + cumulative total); Vehicle owns buffering and downsampling logic.

Offline operation and state reconciliation: If Vehicle operates without Network Link, cumulative throughput must persist locally. Upon reconnection, Vehicle must transmit both the offline-accumulated total and the timestamp range. If external systems cannot reconcile offline periods, measurement integrity is compromised. Recommend: Vehicle maintains persistent log of (timestamp, cumulative_throughput) checkpoints; Telemetry interface includes offline period recovery protocol.

Sensor fault detection and diagnostics: Continuous sensing hardware may degrade (e.g., clogging, drift). If Vehicle does not autonomously detect faults and report them via Telemetry, external systems cannot alert operators. Recommend: Vehicle implements sensor health checks (e.g., rate-of-change bounds, redundant sensor cross-checks) and exports diagnostic flags in Telemetry.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Strong containment (0.85 score justified). The DP is naturally vehicle-resident: sensing hardware, signal conditioning, and integration logic are all physically and logically co-located. Diffusion is minimal and occurs only at well-defined boundaries: (1) Tasking interface (inbound configuration/calibration), (2) Telemetry interface (outbound measurement results). No split responsibility or remote computation. Alternative allocations (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all exhibit high diffusion (0.6–0.8) due to separation of sensing hardware from computation logic, violating locality and introducing latency/reliability risks. Vehicle ownership is architecturally sound.

recommended_revisions: Revise Tasking interface specification to explicitly include calibration command payloads (e.g., sensor offset, scale factor, measurement unit). Define versioning and rollback semantics for calibration updates.

Revise Telemetry interface specification to define: (a) instantaneous flow rate sample rate and aggregation window (e.g., 1 Hz averaged), (b) cumulative throughput reporting frequency, (c) timestamp format and synchronization method (e.g., NTP, GPS, or external clock reference), (d) sensor health/diagnostic flags (e.g., sensor_valid, calibration_age, signal_quality).

Add to Vehicle IM boundary scope: persistent local storage of cumulative throughput checkpoints and offline operation recovery logic. Define reconciliation protocol for Telemetry interface to handle offline periods (e.g., transmit offline_start_time, offline_end_time, offline_cumulative_delta).

Add to Vehicle IM boundary scope: autonomous sensor fault detection (e.g., rate-of-change bounds, redundant sensor cross-checks if available). Export sensor health status in Telemetry to enable external alerting.

Define acceptance test procedure: (1) conduct representative operation with independent ground-truth throughput measurement (e.g., calibrated scale or volumetric tank), (2) compare Vehicle-reported cumulative throughput against ground truth over agreed evaluation period, (3) validate that evaluation period duration matches Tasking input, (4) verify Telemetry timestamps are synchronized within ±1% of evaluation period, (5) test offline operation and reconciliation if applicable.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM coherence and allocation clarity (all three gates pass). However, diffusion_risks expose critical dependencies and interface contracts that are not yet decomposed into sub-FRs. The recommended_revisions identify five distinct architectural concerns: (1) calibration command interface, (2) telemetry interface specification (sampling, aggregation, synchronization), (3) offline operation and state reconciliation, (4) sensor fault detection and diagnostics, (5) acceptance test procedure. These are not implementation details—they are functional requirements that must be explicitly derived and allocated to ensure the DP is realizable without diffusion creep. Safety criticality (high) amplifies the need for explicit fault detection and diagnostics sub-FRs. Proceeding to next-level decomposition is necessary to bind these interface contracts and fault-handling logic before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Accept and apply sensor calibration parameters (offset, scale, unit) via Tasking interface without vehicle retrieval.
FR 1.2: Synchronize evaluation period start/stop with external tasking source and log actual integration window duration for audit.

FR 1.3: Export instantaneous flow rate samples and cumulative throughput via Telemetry interface with explicit sample rate, aggregation strategy, and timestamp synchronization (±1% of evaluation period).
FR 1.4: Maintain persistent local storage of cumulative throughput checkpoints and reconcile offline-accumulated totals upon network reconnection.
FR 1.5: Autonomously detect sensor faults (clogging, drift, signal degradation) and export diagnostic flags (sensor_valid, calibration_age, signal_quality) via Telemetry to enable external alerting.

revise.why: The current DP ('continuous sensing of material stream properties with integration over evaluation period') is logically sound but under-specifies the interface contracts and fault-handling logic required to avoid diffusion. Revise DP to explicitly include: (a) calibration parameter acceptance and application logic, (b) timestamp synchronization and evaluation period binding, (c) telemetry aggregation and export strategy, (d) offline state persistence and reconciliation protocol, (e) sensor health monitoring and diagnostic export. This revision ensures the DP is complete and realizable within the Vehicle IM boundary without external dependencies on interface design.

assumptions: Vehicle has persistent local storage (non-volatile memory) for checkpoint data and offline operation recovery.
Tasking interface can be extended to include calibration command payloads without breaking existing contracts.
Telemetry interface can be extended to include diagnostic flags and timestamp metadata without saturating Network Link (via aggregation/downsampling).
Vehicle clock can be synchronized with external tasking source (NTP, GPS, or explicit marker-based method) to within ±1% of evaluation period.
Sensor hardware supports autonomous health checks (e.g., rate-of-change bounds, redundant cross-checks if dual sensors available).
Acceptance test procedure can be conducted with independent ground-truth measurement (calibrated scale or volumetric tank) during representative operations.
open_risks: Calibration drift correction: If Tasking interface does not support remote calibration commands, sensor drift will require vehicle retrieval. Mitigation: define Tasking payload schema with calibration command type and versioning semantics before detailed design.

Clock synchronization failure: If Vehicle clock cannot be synchronized to ±1% of evaluation period, systematic throughput underreporting will occur. Mitigation: implement NTP-style synchronization or explicit start/stop markers in Tasking interface; log actual integration window duration for audit.

Telemetry bandwidth saturation: High-frequency raw sample streams may exceed Network Link capacity. Mitigation: define aggregation strategy (e.g., 1 Hz averaged samples + cumulative total) and enforce Vehicle-side buffering/downsampling.

Offline operation state loss: If Vehicle loses power or network during offline operation, cumulative throughput checkpoint may be lost. Mitigation: implement periodic checkpoint writes to non-volatile storage; define recovery protocol in Telemetry interface.

Sensor fault detection latency: Autonomous fault detection may lag actual sensor degradation, causing undetected measurement errors. Mitigation: define sensor health check frequency and alert thresholds; require external validation during acceptance testing.

Safety criticality: High safety criticality demands explicit fault detection and diagnostics. If sensor faults are not detected and reported, unsafe throughput underreporting could occur. Mitigation: derive FR 1.5 (sensor fault detection) as mandatory sub-FR; include fault detection in acceptance test procedure.
evidence_needed: Tasking interface specification document with calibration command payload schema, versioning, and rollback semantics.
Telemetry interface specification document with sample rate, aggregation window, timestamp format, synchronization method, and diagnostic flag definitions.
Vehicle IM boundary scope document explicitly including: persistent checkpoint storage, offline operation recovery logic, sensor health monitoring, and diagnostic export.

Acceptance test procedure document with: (1) independent ground-truth measurement method, (2) comparison criteria (e.g., ±5% throughput accuracy), (3) evaluation period duration validation, (4) timestamp synchronization verification (±1% of period), (5) offline operation and reconciliation test cases.
Sensor hardware specification with autonomous health check capabilities (rate-of-change bounds, redundant cross-checks, diagnostic output).
Network Link capacity analysis confirming that Telemetry aggregation strategy (1 Hz averaged samples + cumulative total + diagnostic flags) does not exceed available bandwidth.
Clock synchronization method specification (NTP, GPS, or marker-based) with achievable accuracy bounds and failure modes.
